speaker: interviewer
text: I'm conducting an interview with Arnold Greenblatt. My name is Mark Bernstein. And this is tape number five. I'm sorry, so I was just asking you how it affected your religious beliefs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, In fact, it is very bad. I was-- three times a year through Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur up until my father passed away. And when I came to America, of course, my father-- I explained to him my feelings on what we've been through and how mother died. That affected me terribly. Not only me, the fact that my younger sister was. Then it affected me. And we adjusted to it, like Saturdays. I never forced my children, you know, to-- of course I wanted them to get a Jewish education. But when they said they don't want no more, I let it go at that. I never forced them to do it. And of course they know that they're Jewish. When my father passed away, I went to Kaddish every day in the morning and at night. And my greatest ambition was, because I respected him so much, to go to Europe and bring out a Sefer Torah from the shul that he loved and give it to my shul in his name to be there. And I said, if I accomplished that, I think I don't care if I die tomorrow. And I accomplished it 10 years ago, and I feel very good about it. My father is buried here in Flushing. I go three, four times a year. I get up Sunday morning sometimes, just decide I'm going to the-- to the cemetery. I take care of the shrubs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And just to-- just to be there to say [NON-ENGLISH] and say Kaddish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I-- I came back. I must say, you know, I love my shul. And I believe in God. And I just hope that my-- everything goes well after I'm gone for my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effect have your experiences had on the way you've raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't believe it had any effect. The children were raised Jews. They knew that they have to marry Jews. I stressed that point even to my grandchildren, that I will not-- and I repeat, I will not-- tolerate out-of-the-religion marriage for my family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I want my grandchildren to see this tape and to hear what I just said.

speaker: interviewer
text: But apart from religious things, do you think it affected the way you brought them up in any other way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, well, it may have a factor in the sense that I didn't bring them as the orthodox way, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because I couldn't preach them something that I wasn't myself, you know, that I believe in myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have dreams about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things do you dream about.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only dreams I don't have is I wish I could dream about my mother a little more. I dream working. I wake up in a cold sweat, you know, working in-- in a farm in the front lines, and the journey, the three days and nights. Like, I'll give you an example. I can't watch-- there used to be a program on television, 'Wagon Train.' It's a Western program where people travel on horse and wagons, and they go through mud and rain and stuff. And my kids, my nephews used to watch it. I can't watch that on television, because that brings back memories of my ordeal. When the people are going west in the old days, on horse and wagons, I had the same ordeal on the horse and wagons and blown up bridges and mudholes and water. My horse couldn't pull the wagon enough, and I was behind it pushing it. I'm a human being. I'm not an animal. I had animal powers in me. I don't know where they came from.

speaker: interviewer
text: And finally is there a message from your experiences that you would like to leave for future generations or for your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be good, decent citizens, honest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father told me this once. Son, always be a leader, never a follower and be honest-- and that's why I am where I am today, because I'm an honest person and a sincere person and a good-hearted person-- and to be decent citizens, and to be good to their-- hopefully someday they'll have children-- and to be good to them, and to raise them properly, and show them a lot of love, that I left-- that I showed to my children. I have a picture of-- of my younger daughter that she gave me since she was a teenager. And she wrote, Daddy, it says, I don't know how to thank you for the things you did for me. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I just hope that they raise their children, and they live a clean, normal life and don't marry out of the religion, because there's always going to be instant coffee. It'll-- It'll never work. You've got to stick to what you are and be proud, be very proud. And especially to my grandchildren, I say to be proud that you had a grandfather that loved you so much and still loves you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is-- is my wife getting in today?

speaker: interviewer
text: In a little. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Debrecen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's a family portrait. From right to left is my mother, Sarah Greenblatt. Black And my name-- I am the next one, Arnold Greenblatt, then my older brother, Ernie Greenblatt, my older sister in the back, Clara, and my younger sister, holding the ball, is Judy. And the last person is my father, Ignatz Greenblatt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Debrecen when I was two years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you manage to get this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found that picture when I came back from a concentration camp. I found it in the ghetto, scattered on the floor in a room that we lived in. That's where I found it. This picture is a maid that used to be our maid when I was 12 or 13 years old. My mother used to send her to the beauty parlor to have her hair done and dressed her very nicely. She was a farmer's daughter. And when the Nazis came in and Jews weren't allowed to employ Gentiles, she had to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Betty. This is our first passport picture when we tried to come to America-- myself on the right, my mother in the middle, and my younger sister on the left side, with the three of us on one passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And how did you get this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found it-- the pictures were all found in the ghetto. This is a picture of myself when I was 13 years old at bar mitzvah in our yard holding a little torah with a tallit on my shoulder, also found in the ghetto among the other pictures. This is a picture of myself, the motorcycle that I used to ride. This was taken in the yard in front of our bedroom window at the age of 14 and found in the ghetto. This picture was taken a few weeks after I came back from the concentration camp. As you can see, I lost a lot of weight, and it was taken when-- after the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Debrecen in the yard where we-- where we lived. This picture was taken in 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was on my license when I drove the truck for American Joint Distribution Committee. It was taken after the war, 1945. This picture, or scripture, was given to me by the congregation in Debrecen in memory of my mother. When I came back from concentration camp, I repaired the lights in a synagogue. And I did not take any money for it, and it was cut into the door of the electrical box in the vestibule of the synagogue in Debrecen. When I was back there three years ago-- three years ago, I found it cracked in half and out of the-- the door, the frame. And I asked the caretaker if I can have it as a memento, and they let me bring it home. It just says that I did the lights and my mother's name and the [INAUDIBLE]. This picture is three European boys, that we became friends. I only remember the one on the left. His name is [? Freddie ?] [? Milbauer. ?] He is the fellow that introduced me to my wife. I am in the center. The fellow on the right-- I don't know. I don't remember his name. I haven't seen him in 30 years or more. This was taken here when we came to America, in 1947. This is my wedding picture. That happened 48 years ago. On October the 22nd, it will be 48 years that this picture was taken. That's my wife, Betty, and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brooklyn. This is the picture of my daughter, Susan, and my father at my daughter's wedding, where my father-- my father gave their blessing to the bride, 26-- 25 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: At [? Cavelson ?] Caterers in Brooklyn. This picture was taken in Debrecen three years ago. This is the two towers of the Roman Catholic church that I remember as a child. There's a park around it. And I used to run around there. This is the synagogue in Debrecen, in Hungary. It was taken three years ago. Unfortunately, it's not being used. There's not enough Jews to use it. This is where I repaired the lights. And this is where that little plaque came from-- from the vestibule of this synagogue. This is the same synagogue that you just saw, except the inside. And the lights are still hanging. And that's my brother, standing in the row where my father used to sit as a memorial. Ladies went upstairs, because you could see the balcony. It was a beautiful synagogue in Debrecen. This is the grave of my mother. This picture was taken three years ago. That's my brother and I standing there. And the stone has been changed since then. On this-- this grave there's no Hungarian writing. And on the new one, her name is written out in Hungary. This stone is 50 years old. This picture was taken on my grandson's bar mitzvah in Brooklyn, right here in the neighborhood. On the right side it's myself. Next to me is my wife, Betty. She's holding her hand on my grandson, the bar mitzvah boy. Behind him is his father. Next to him is my granddaughter, Laura. Behind her is Evan's mother, Susan. Next her is his sister, Ruth. In front of her is my other grandson, Jason. And next to her is her husband, Marty.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- what was the occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the bar mitzvah [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] five years ago. This is a picture of myself at the Temple Hillel in Brooklyn, where I was-- I served four years-- two term-- presidency. After the services, I usually greeted the congregation. This is on the [NON-ENGLISH] Kaddish. This is a picture of myself, my two grandsons, and the Torah that I had brought from Europe. It was dedicated to be taken into the shul. And I gave my grandson the honor to do it, so that he'll have something to remember. This was 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken on Ralph Avenue in Brooklyn at Temple Hillel. This is myself at work. Six years ago, this picture was taken, possibly seven. But I'm building an air pollution control equipment in Brooklyn at the Sternvent Company, where I worked for 34 years as an assembly manager. This is a plaque I received six years ago from the company that I was for 34 years-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and with a gold watch, 34 years of dedicated service. This is a picture of myself and my three grandchildren. On the right is Jason Miller, then myself. On the front is Lauren Weisler and Evan Miller.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken six months ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In their house in New Jersey. This picture was taken about a month ago in New Jersey at my daughter's house. From right to left is my sister Clara Thaw, who right now presides in Florida. Next to her is my wife, Betty. And next to her is my brother, Ernie Greenblatt. Next to him is his wife, Anita. And sitting down in the other chair is my younger sister, Judy Stevens that lives in California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken about a month ago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My-- my wife is in the picture. She couldn't be here in person because she's not feeling well today.
